title: Partition k subsets
description: Partition array into k equal subsets

examples.explanation: Total sum = 20, target = 5. Sorted descending: [5,4,3,3,2,2,1]. Initialize subset_sums = [0,0,0,0]. Backtrack idx=0 (element 5): try subset 0: add 5 -> [5,0,0,0]; backtrack idx=1 (4): try subset 0: 5+4>5 skip; subset 1: add 4 -> [5,4,0,0]; backtrack idx=2 (3): subset 0: 5+3>5 skip; subset 1: 4+3>5 skip; subset 2: add 3 -> [5,4,3,0]; backtrack idx=3 (3): subset 0: skip; subset 1: skip; subset 2: 3+3>5 skip; subset 3: add 3 -> [5,4,3,3]; backtrack idx=4 (2): subset 0: skip; subset 1: 4+2>5 skip; subset 2: 3+2=5 -> [5,4,5,3]; backtrack idx=5 (2): subset 0: skip; subset 1: 4+2>5 skip; subset 2: 5+2>5 skip; subset 3: 3+2=5 -> [5,4,5,5]; backtrack idx=6 (1): subset 0: 5+1>5 skip; subset 1: 4+1=5 -> [5,5,5,5]; backtrack idx=7: all subsets =5, return true.
Total sum = 10, not divisible by 3, return false immediately without backtracking.

approaches.name: Brute Force Backtracking O(k^n)
approaches.summary: Try all possible assignments of each element to one of k subsets, backtracking when a subset exceeds the target sum. It's straightforward but extremely slow for large inputs, suitable only for small n and k.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Partition Array into K Equal Sum Subsets via Brute Force Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of brute force backtracking to partition an array into k subsets with equal sums.
ladders.explanation: The brute force backtracking approach systematically explores all possible assignments of each array element to one of k subsets, backtracking when a subset's sum exceeds the target. The intuition is to treat each element as a choice point: for each element, try placing it in each subset that hasn't exceeded the target sum, then recursively process the next element. This exhaustive search guarantees finding a valid partition if one exists, but at the cost of exponential time complexity. The algorithm begins by checking if the total sum is divisible by k and if any element exceeds the target sum—if so, return false immediately. Then sort the array in descending order to place larger elements first, which helps prune branches earlier. The recursive backtracking function takes the current index in the array and an array of current subset sums. For the current element, iterate through all subsets: if adding the element to a subset doesn't exceed the target, add it, then recursively call for the next index. If the recursion returns true, propagate true upward; otherwise, remove the element from that subset (backtrack) and try the next subset. Base cases: if all elements are placed (index == n), check if all subsets have exactly the target sum—return true if so. Optimization: skip subsets with the same sum as previous subsets to avoid duplicate permutations (since subsets are indistinguishable). Time complexity is O(k^n) in the worst case, as each of n elements can go into any of k subsets, though pruning reduces this somewhat. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth and the subset sums array. Connection to L1: This level integrates the backtracking subroutine with preprocessing and optimization to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include not sorting descending (leading to slower pruning), not skipping duplicate subset sums (causing redundant work), and forgetting to check divisibility upfront. Real code pattern: def canPartitionKSubsets(nums, k): total = sum(nums); if total % k != 0: return False; target = total // k; nums.sort(reverse=True); if nums[0] > target: return False; subset_sums = [0]*k; def backtrack(idx): if idx == len(nums): return all(s == target for s in subset_sums); for i in range(k): if subset_sums[i] + nums[idx] <= target: subset_sums[i] += nums[idx]; if backtrack(idx+1): return True; subset_sums[i] -= nums[idx]; if subset_sums[i] == 0: break; return False; return backtrack(0).

examples.trace: Total sum = 20, target = 5. Sorted descending: [5,4,3,3,2,2,1]. Initialize subset_sums = [0,0,0,0]. Backtrack idx=0 (element 5): try subset 0: add 5 -> [5,0,0,0]; backtrack idx=1 (4): try subset 0: 5+4>5 skip; subset 1: add 4 -> [5,4,0,0]; backtrack idx=2 (3): subset 0: 5+3>5 skip; subset 1: 4+3>5 skip; subset 2: add 3 -> [5,4,3,0]; backtrack idx=3 (3): subset 0: skip; subset 1: skip; subset 2: 3+3>5 skip; subset 3: add 3 -> [5,4,3,3]; backtrack idx=4 (2): subset 0: skip; subset 1: 4+2>5 skip; subset 2: 3+2=5 -> [5,4,5,3]; backtrack idx=5 (2): subset 0: skip; subset 1: 4+2>5 skip; subset 2: 5+2>5 skip; subset 3: 3+2=5 -> [5,4,5,5]; backtrack idx=6 (1): subset 0: 5+1>5 skip; subset 1: 4+1=5 -> [5,5,5,5]; backtrack idx=7: all subsets =5, return true.
Total sum = 10, not divisible by 3, return false immediately without backtracking.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking Assignment of Elements to Subsets
ladders.desc: Recursive backtracking function that assigns array elements to k subsets while tracking sums.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the brute force approach: a recursive function that attempts to place each array element into one of k subsets, backtracking when a subset's sum exceeds the target. The intuition is to explore all possible distributions of elements across subsets via depth-first search, abandoning paths that violate the sum constraint. The function takes parameters: the current index in the array (starting from 0), an array of current subset sums (initialized to zeros), the target sum per subset, and the original sorted array. For the element at the current index, loop through each subset from 0 to k-1. If adding the element to that subset would not exceed the target, add it to the subset sum, then recursively call the function for the next index. If that recursive call returns true (meaning a valid partition was found), propagate true back up. If false, backtrack by subtracting the element from that subset sum and try the next subset. Base case: if the current index equals the array length (all elements placed), check if all subset sums equal the target—return true if so, false otherwise. Optimization: skip subsets with the same current sum as a previous subset to avoid duplicate assignments (since subsets are unlabeled). Time complexity for this subroutine alone is O(k^n) in worst case, as it explores up to k choices per element. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called within the full solution after preprocessing. Common mistakes include not resetting subset sums after backtracking, infinite recursion from missing base case, and not optimizing with duplicate sum skipping. Real code pattern: def backtrack(idx, subset_sums): if idx == len(nums): return all(s == target for s in subset_sums); for i in range(k): if subset_sums[i] + nums[idx] <= target: subset_sums[i] += nums[idx]; if backtrack(idx+1, subset_sums): return True; subset_sums[i] -= nums[idx]; if subset_sums[i] == 0: break; return False.

examples.trace: Backtrack idx=0 (element 3): try subset 0: add 3 -> [3,0]; backtrack idx=1 (2): subset 0: 3+2>3 skip; subset 1: add 2 -> [3,2]; backtrack idx=2 (1): subset 0: 3+1>3 skip; subset 1: 2+1=3 -> [3,3]; backtrack idx=3: all subsets =3, return true.
Backtrack idx=0 (element 4): try subset 0: 4>2.5 skip; subset 1: 4>2.5 skip; return false (no valid placement).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pruning Search with Subset Sum Constraints
ladders.desc: The technique of abandoning search paths when a subset's sum exceeds the target, central to backtracking efficiency.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves pruning the search space during backtracking by enforcing that no subset's sum exceeds the target value. The intuition is that if adding an element to a subset would make its sum greater than the target, that assignment cannot lead to a valid solution (since all subsets must have exactly the target sum), so we skip it and try other options. This reduces the number of recursive calls significantly compared to a naive exhaustive search. In practice, during the backtracking loop over subsets, before adding an element to a subset, check if the current subset sum plus the element is less than or equal to the target. If it is, proceed; otherwise, skip that subset. This check is performed for each element at each recursive level. Additionally, an optimization is applied: if after backtracking from a subset, its sum returns to zero, break out of the loop to avoid duplicate permutations (since subsets are unlabeled, placing an element in an empty subset after failing in another empty subset is redundant). This logic is what makes backtracking feasible even for moderate inputs, though it still results in exponential time. Time complexity analysis: without pruning, it would be exactly k^n; with pruning, it's still O(k^n) but with a lower constant factor. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion. Connection to L1: This pruning is implemented within the backtracking subroutine to limit exploration. Common mistakes include incorrect inequality (using < instead of <=), not resetting sums after pruning, and missing the optimization break. Real code pattern: for i in range(k): if subset_sums[i] + nums[idx] <= target: subset_sums[i] += nums[idx]; if backtrack(idx+1): return True; subset_sums[i] -= nums[idx]; if subset_sums[i] == 0: break.

examples.trace: Element 5 at idx=0: try subset 0: 0+5<=5 -> add, sums=[5,0]; backtrack idx=1 (3): subset 0: 5+3>5 prune, skip; subset 1: 0+3<=5 -> add, sums=[5,3]; backtrack idx=2 (2): subset 0: 5+2>5 prune; subset 1: 3+2=5 -> add, sums=[5,5]; idx=3: all=5, return true.
Element 6 at idx=0: subset 0: 0+6>3.5 prune; subset 1: 0+6>3.5 prune; no valid placements, return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Tree Traversal with State
ladders.desc: Implementing depth-first search recursion that maintains state (subset sums) across calls.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating a recursive function that traverses a decision tree, where each node represents an assignment of an array element to a subset, and the state (current subset sums) is passed and modified through recursive calls. The intuition is to model the problem as a tree: at each level of recursion (corresponding to an array element), there are up to k branches (choices of subset). The state—an array of subset sums—accumulates choices as we go deeper. Recursion naturally implements depth-first search: try one branch fully, then backtrack to try others. The function must: 1) Accept state parameters (e.g., current index and subset sums). 2) Have a base case that checks if all elements are processed. 3) In the recursive case, iterate through possible choices, update state, recurse, then revert state (backtrack). This pattern is common in backtracking algorithms. Time complexity for this block in isolation depends on the number of recursive calls, but conceptually it's about managing state across calls. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and state storage. Connection to L2: This traversal mechanism is where pruning is applied during the iteration over choices. Common mistakes include not copying state (leading to shared modifications), forgetting to revert state after recursion, and stack overflow from deep recursion. Real code pattern: def dfs(idx, sums): if idx == n: return check(sums); for choice in choices: if valid(choice, sums): update(sums, choice); if dfs(idx+1, sums): return True; revert(sums, choice); return False.

examples.input: n = 2, choices = [0,1] (subset indices), state = [0,0], idx = 0, elements = [3,2], target = 2.5
examples.trace: dfs idx=0: try choice 0: sums=[3,0]; dfs idx=1: try choice 0: sums=[5,0] invalid (>2.5); choice 1: sums=[3,2]; dfs idx=2: base case, sums not all 2.5, return false; backtrack: revert sums to [3,0]; try choice 1: sums=[0,3]; dfs idx=1: choice 0: sums=[2,3]; dfs idx=2: false; choice 1: sums=[0,5] invalid; return false.

examples.input: n = 1, choices = [0], state = [0], idx = 0, elements = [5], target = 5
examples.trace: dfs idx=0: choice 0: sums=[5]; dfs idx=1: base case, sums all 5, return true.

testCases.input: n = 2, choices = [0,1], state = [0,0], idx = 0, elements = [3,2], target = 2.5
n = 1, choices = [0], state = [0], idx = 0, elements = [5], target = 5
n = 3, choices = [0,1,2], state = [0,0,0], idx = 0, elements = [1,1,1], target = 1
n = 2, choices = [0,1], state = [0,0], idx = 0, elements = [4,1], target = 2.5
n = 2, choices = [0,1], state = [0,0], idx = 0, elements = [2,2], target = 2
n = 3, choices = [0,1], state = [0,0], idx = 0, elements = [3,2,1], target = 3
n = 1, choices = [0,1], state = [0,0], idx = 0, elements = [0], target = 0
n = 2, choices = [0], state = [0], idx = 0, elements = [6,6], target = 6
n = 3, choices = [0,1,2], state = [0,0,0], idx = 0, elements = [5,5,5], target = 5
n = 2, choices = [0,1], state = [0,0], idx = 0, elements = [7,3], target = 5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating and Updating an Array of Subset Sums
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of looping through subsets and adding/subtracting elements to track sums.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves manipulating an array that holds the current sum of each subset. The intuition is to maintain a dynamic record of how much total has been assigned to each subset so far, which is essential for enforcing the sum constraint. The array is initialized with zeros (no elements assigned). For each element, we iterate through the array indices (0 to k-1) representing subsets. For each subset, we check if adding the element would exceed the target: if subset_sums[i] + element <= target. If valid, we update the array: subset_sums[i] += element. After recursing, we revert the update: subset_sums[i] -= element (backtracking). This add/subtract pattern is core to state management in backtracking. Additionally, we optimize by breaking the loop if after backtracking the subset sum becomes zero, to avoid duplicate work (since subsets are indistinguishable). Time complexity for this operation per element is O(k) for the loop, contributing to the overall O(k^n). Space complexity is O(k) for the array. Connection to L3: This operation is performed within the recursive traversal to modify state. Common mistakes include using the wrong index, not checking the inequality correctly, or forgetting to revert changes. Real code pattern: for i in range(k): if subset_sums[i] + nums[idx] <= target: subset_sums[i] += nums[idx]; # recurse; subset_sums[i] -= nums[idx]; if subset_sums[i] == 0: break.

examples.trace: Loop i=0: check 3+2=5 <=5 -> add, sums=[5,1]; after recursion, subtract, sums back to [3,1]; i=1: check 1+2=3 <=5 -> add, sums=[3,3]; after recursion, subtract, sums back to [3,1]; since sums[1] became 0? No, it's 1, so no break.
Loop i=0: 0+4=4 >3 skip; i=1: 0+4=4 >3 skip; no additions made.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Summing Array Elements and Comparing to a Target
ladders.desc: Basic arithmetic of calculating sums and checking if they meet a condition.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves two simple operations: summing numbers and comparing sums to a target value. The intuition is that partitioning into equal subsets requires knowing the total sum of all elements and ensuring each subset's sum matches a target (total divided by k). First, summing: given an array of numbers, add them all together to get a total. This can be done with a loop or built-in sum function. Second, comparing: for a subset, check if its current sum plus a new element is less than or equal to the target. This uses basic inequality operations. These operations are prerequisites for the backtracking algorithm, as they determine feasibility of assignments. For example, if total sum is not divisible by k, partitioning is impossible. Similarly, during backtracking, we constantly check if adding an element keeps the subset sum within bounds. Time complexity for summing is O(n) for n elements; comparing is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for variables. Connection to L4: These operations are used when updating subset sums and checking constraints. Common mistakes include integer division errors, off-by-one in loops, or misunderstanding inequality (e.g., using < instead of <=). Real code pattern: total = sum(nums); if total % k != 0: return False; target = total // k; and in loop: if current_sum + element <= target: proceed.

examples.trace: Sum elements: 1+2=3, +3=6, +4=10. Total=10. Check divisibility: 10 % 2 = 0, so target = 10 / 2 = 5.
Sum: 5+5=10, +6=16. Total=16. 16 % 3 = 1 (not 0), so partitioning impossible.

approaches.name: Optimized Backtracking with Sorting and Pruning O(n * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Sort the array in descending order and use backtracking to assign elements to subsets, pruning early if a subset cannot reach the target sum. This reduces the search space significantly compared to brute force.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Partition Array into K Equal Subsets with Optimized Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete solution using backtracking with sorting and pruning to partition array into k subsets with equal sums.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires partitioning an array of integers into k subsets such that each subset has exactly the same sum. The optimized backtracking approach works by first checking if the total sum is divisible by k and if any element exceeds the target subset sum (which would make partitioning impossible). We sort the array in descending order to place larger elements first, which helps prune invalid branches early. We maintain an array to track the current sum of each subset. The backtracking function tries to place each element into one of the k subsets. If adding an element to a subset would exceed the target sum, we skip that placement. If a subset reaches exactly the target sum, we move to the next subset. Key pruning techniques include: 1) Skipping duplicate elements when the previous identical element wasn't placed in the current subset (to avoid redundant permutations), 2) Starting with empty subsets and only creating new subsets when necessary (since subsets are indistinguishable), and 3) Early termination if any subset cannot reach the target sum. The time complexity is O(n * 2^n) in worst case due to exploring all possible assignments, but pruning reduces this significantly in practice. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and subset sums array. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete backtracking logic that uses the key subroutine for element placement decisions.

examples.trace:
1. Total sum = 20, target = 20/4 = 5. All elements ≤ 5, proceed.
2. Sort descending: [5,4,3,3,2,2,1]
3. Initialize subset sums = [0,0,0,0]
4. Backtrack:
   - Place 5 in subset 0 → sums = [5,0,0,0]
   - Place 4 in subset 1 → sums = [5,4,0,0]
   - Place 3 in subset 1 → sums = [5,7,0,0] (exceeds 5, backtrack)
   - Place 3 in subset 2 → sums = [5,4,3,0]
   - Place 3 (second) in subset 2 → sums = [5,4,6,0] (exceeds, backtrack)
   - Place 3 (second) in subset 3 → sums = [5,4,3,3]
   - Place 2 in subset 1 → sums = [5,6,3,3] (exceeds, backtrack)
   - Place 2 in subset 2 → sums = [5,4,5,3]
   - Place 2 (second) in subset 3 → sums = [5,4,5,5]
   - Place 1 in subset 1 → sums = [5,5,5,5] → all subsets reach target 5
5. Return true

1. Total sum = 10, target = 10/3 ≈ 3.33 (not integer) → return false immediately
2. No further computation needed

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking Function for Subset Assignment
ladders.desc: The recursive backtracking function that assigns elements to subsets while pruning invalid paths.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the optimized backtracking approach. It takes the current index in the sorted array, the array of current subset sums, and tries to place the current element into one of the k subsets. The intuition is to explore all possible assignments but prune branches where: 1) Adding the element would exceed the target subset sum, 2) The current subset sum is 0 and we've already tried placing elements in previous empty subsets (since subsets are indistinguishable), and 3) We encounter duplicate elements where the previous identical element wasn't placed in this subset (avoiding redundant permutations). The function returns true if it successfully places all elements (reaches the end of the array). The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Base case: if index == n, return true (all elements placed). 2) For each subset from 0 to k-1: a) If subset sum + nums[index] > target, continue to next subset. b) If subset sum == 0 and we've processed previous empty subsets, break (prune). c) If nums[index] == nums[index-1] and we didn't place the previous element in this subset, continue (prune duplicates). d) Add element to subset, recursively call with index+1. e) If recursive call returns true, return true. f) Remove element from subset (backtrack). 3) Return false if no placement works. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(k^n) without pruning, but with pruning it's much better. Space is O(n) for recursion. This connects to L0 as the core recursive function, and to L2 as it implements the pruning logic.

examples.input: nums = [5,4,3], target = 5, k = 2, start with sums = [0,0], index = 0
examples.trace:
1. index=0, nums[0]=5
   Try subset 0: 0+5=5 ≤ target → sums=[5,0], recurse index=1
     index=1, nums[1]=4
     Try subset 0: 5+4=9 >5 → skip
     Try subset 1: 0+4=4 ≤5 → sums=[5,4], recurse index=2
       index=2, nums[2]=3
       Try subset 0: 5+3=8 >5 → skip
       Try subset 1: 4+3=7 >5 → skip
       Return false → backtrack sums=[5,0]
   Try subset 1: 0+5=5 ≤5 → sums=[0,5], recurse index=1
     index=1, nums[1]=4
     Try subset 0: 0+4=4 ≤5 → sums=[4,5], recurse index=2
       index=2, nums[2]=3
       Try subset 0: 4+3=7 >5 → skip
       Try subset 1: 5+3=8 >5 → skip
       Return false → backtrack sums=[0,5]
     Try subset 1: 5+4=9 >5 → skip
     Return false
2. Return false (but note: with full array [5,4,3,2,1] and k=3 it would work)

examples.input: nums = [3,2,2], target = 4, k = 2, start with sums = [0,0], index = 0
examples.trace:
1. index=0, nums[0]=3
   Try subset 0: 0+3=3 ≤4 → sums=[3,0], recurse index=1
     index=1, nums[1]=2
     Try subset 0: 3+2=5 >4 → skip
     Try subset 1: 0+2=2 ≤4 → sums=[3,2], recurse index=2
       index=2, nums[2]=2
       Try subset 0: 3+2=5 >4 → skip
       Try subset 1: 2+2=4 ≤4 → sums=[3,4], recurse index=3
         index=3 == n → return true
2. Return true

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pruning Techniques for Backtracking Efficiency
ladders.desc: The key pruning strategies that reduce the search space in backtracking.
ladders.explanation: Pruning is essential to make backtracking feasible for this problem. The core insight is that we can eliminate many branches without exploring them fully. Three main pruning techniques: 1) **Sorting in descending order**: By placing larger elements first, we quickly identify if a subset exceeds the target sum, pruning deeper branches early. If we placed small elements first, we might waste time exploring many combinations only to fail later when adding a large element. 2) **Skipping duplicate elements**: When consecutive elements are equal, if we didn't place the first one in a particular subset, placing the second one in that same subset would lead to identical permutations. We skip to avoid redundant work. 3) **Empty subset pruning**: Since subsets are indistinguishable (e.g., subset 0 and subset 1 are interchangeable), if we're trying to place an element in an empty subset and we've already tried placing elements in previous empty subsets, we can break. This prevents exploring symmetric solutions. The intuition is that the order of subsets doesn't matter, so we only need to consider placing elements in the first empty subset we encounter. Time complexity improves from O(k^n) to roughly O(n * 2^n) with these prunings. Space remains O(n). This connects to L1 as these prunings are implemented in the backtracking function, and to L3 as it builds on the concept of backtracking with constraints.

examples.input: Sorted array [5,4,3,2,1], target = 5, k = 3, demonstrating descending sort pruning
examples.trace:
Without sorting: Try placing 1 first, then 2, etc. Many combinations like [1,2] (sum=3), [1,2,3] (sum=6 >5) fail late.
With descending sort [5,4,3,2,1]:
- Place 5 in subset 0 → sum=5 (complete)
- Place 4 in subset 1 → sum=4
- Try 3 in subset 1 → 4+3=7 >5 → prune immediately
- Instead place 3 in subset 2 → sum=3
- Continue...
Result: Early pruning when large elements exceed target.

examples.input: Array [3,3,2,2] with duplicates, target = 5, k = 2, demonstrating duplicate pruning
examples.trace:
Sorted: [3,3,2,2]
Without duplicate pruning:
- Place first 3 in subset 0, second 3 in subset 0 → invalid
- Place first 3 in subset 0, second 3 in subset 1 → explore
- Place first 3 in subset 1, second 3 in subset 0 → symmetric to previous, redundant
With duplicate pruning:
- After placing first 3 in subset 0, when placing second 3, skip subset 0 (since first 3 wasn't placed in subset 0? Wait, correction: if nums[i] == nums[i-1] and we didn't place nums[i-1] in current subset, skip)
- Actually, example: if first 3 placed in subset 0, second 3: check if we placed first 3 in subset 0? Yes, so we can try subset 0. But if we didn't place first 3 in subset 0, skip subset 0 for second 3.
- This avoids exploring [subset0:3, subset1:3] and [subset0:3, subset1:3] as different orders.

testCases.input: Array [6,5,4], target = 7, k = 2, should pruning occur?
Array [4,4,2,2], target = 6, k = 2, test duplicate pruning
Array [1,1,1,1], target = 2, k = 2, test empty subset pruning
Array [10,1,1], target = 6, k = 2, test sort pruning
Array [3,3,3], target = 3, k = 3, test multiple prunings
Array [5,2,2,1], target = 5, k = 2, test combined pruning
Array [7,3,3,1], target = 7, k = 2, test early termination
Array [4,4,4,4], target = 8, k = 2, test duplicate handling
Array [6,2,2,2], target = 6, k = 2, test empty subset rule
Array [9,5,4], target = 9, k = 2, test large element first

ladders.title: Building Block — Backtracking with Constraints
ladders.desc: Implementing backtracking to assign items to groups with a sum constraint.
ladders.explanation: This building block introduces backtracking in the context of assigning elements to groups (subsets) such that each group's sum does not exceed a target value. The intuition is to systematically explore all possible assignments but only continue along paths that satisfy the constraint. We maintain an array tracking the current sum of each group. For each element, we try placing it in each group. If placing it would cause the group's sum to exceed the target, we skip that group. If we successfully place all elements, we've found a valid assignment. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Base case: If all elements placed (index == n), return true. 2) For each group from 0 to k-1: a) If group sum + current element > target, continue to next group. b) Add element to group, update sum. c) Recursively try to place next element. d) If recursion returns true, return true. e) Remove element from group (backtrack). 3) Return false if no group works. Time complexity is O(k^n) without pruning, as each element has k choices. Space is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L2 as it's the basic backtracking framework that pruning techniques optimize, and to L4 as it uses array manipulation to track sums.

examples.input: nums = [3,2,1], target = 3, k = 2, groups sums = [0,0], index = 0
examples.trace:
1. index=0, element=3
   Try group 0: 0+3=3 ≤3 → sums=[3,0], recurse index=1
     index=1, element=2
     Try group 0: 3+2=5 >3 → skip
     Try group 1: 0+2=2 ≤3 → sums=[3,2], recurse index=2
       index=2, element=1
       Try group 0: 3+1=4 >3 → skip
       Try group 1: 2+1=3 ≤3 → sums=[3,3], recurse index=3
         index=3 == n → return true
2. Return true

examples.input: nums = [4,3,2], target = 4, k = 2, groups sums = [0,0], index = 0
examples.trace:
1. index=0, element=4
   Try group 0: 0+4=4 ≤4 → sums=[4,0], recurse index=1
     index=1, element=3
     Try group 0: 4+3=7 >4 → skip
     Try group 1: 0+3=3 ≤4 → sums=[4,3], recurse index=2
       index=2, element=2
       Try group 0: 4+2=6 >4 → skip
       Try group 1: 3+2=5 >4 → skip
       Return false → backtrack sums=[4,0]
   Try group 1: 0+4=4 ≤4 → sums=[0,4], recurse index=1
     index=1, element=3
     Try group 0: 0+3=3 ≤4 → sums=[3,4], recurse index=2
       index=2, element=2
       Try group 0: 3+2=5 >4 → skip
       Try group 1: 4+2=6 >4 → skip
       Return false
2. Return false

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Summation and Comparison
ladders.desc: Calculating sums of array elements and comparing against a target value.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves computing the sum of elements in an array or subarray and checking if it equals, exceeds, or is less than a target value. The intuition is that partitioning into equal subsets requires knowing the total sum to determine the target subset sum, and during backtracking, we need to track partial sums to ensure they don't exceed the target. The step-by-step process: 1) **Total sum calculation**: Iterate through all elements, accumulating their values. Time O(n), space O(1). 2) **Target computation**: Divide total sum by k, check if divisible (total % k == 0). 3) **Partial sum tracking**: Maintain an array of k integers representing current sums of each subset. When adding an element, update the corresponding sum. 4) **Comparison**: Check if current sum + element > target (prune), or if current sum == target (subset complete). Common mistakes include integer division errors (use integer arithmetic), not checking for overflow (though rare with typical constraints), and incorrect indexing when updating sums. Real code pattern: total = sum(nums); target = total // k; if total % k != 0: return False; sums = [0] * k; if sums[i] + num > target: continue. This connects to L3 as it provides the basic arithmetic needed for constraint checking in backtracking, and to L5 as it builds on simple array iteration.

examples.trace:
1. Initialize total = 0
2. Add 4 → total = 4
3. Add 3 → total = 7
4. Add 2 → total = 9
5. Add 1 → total = 10
6. target = 10 / 2 = 5
7. Check 10 % 2 == 0 → true, proceed

examples.input: Current sums = [3,2], target = 5, adding element = 3 to subset 0
examples.trace:
1. sums[0] = 3
2. element = 3
3. new_sum = sums[0] + 3 = 6
4. Compare new_sum (6) > target (5) → true
5. Therefore, cannot add element to subset 0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Iteration and Basic Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Iterating through arrays and performing basic arithmetic operations.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves two core skills: 1) **Array iteration**: Accessing each element in an array sequentially using a loop. In Python-style, this is typically done with a for loop: for num in nums: # process num. The intuition is that arrays store multiple values, and we often need to examine or modify each one. Key points: know the array length (len(nums)), access elements by index (nums[i]), and understand zero-based indexing. 2) **Basic arithmetic**: Performing addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, and modulo operations on integers. For this problem, addition accumulates sums, division computes targets, and modulo checks divisibility. Important details: integer division (//) truncates decimals, modulo (%) gives remainder, and order of operations matters. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loops, confusing += with =, and incorrect use of division (using / instead of // for integers). Real code pattern: total = 0; for num in nums: total += num; target = total // k; if total % k != 0: return False. This connects to L4 as it provides the basic tools needed for sum calculations and comparisons, and is essential for any array-based algorithm.

examples.trace:
1. Initialize sum = 0
2. First iteration: num = 2, sum = 0 + 2 = 2
3. Second iteration: num = 4, sum = 2 + 4 = 6
4. Third iteration: num = 6, sum = 6 + 6 = 12
5. Final sum = 12

examples.input: Total sum = 15, k = 4, check divisibility and compute target
examples.trace:
1. Compute target: 15 // 4 = 3 (integer division)
2. Compute remainder: 15 % 4 = 3
3. Since remainder != 0, 15 is not divisible by 4
4. Therefore, cannot partition into 4 equal subsets

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Bitmask O(n * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Use bitmask DP to represent subsets of elements and track sums modulo target, iteratively building valid partitions. This avoids recursion overhead and is more efficient for medium-sized n by leveraging memoization.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Partition Array into K Equal Subsets Using Bitmask DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using bitmask DP to determine if array can be partitioned into k subsets with equal sums.
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires checking if an array of integers can be partitioned into k subsets where each subset has exactly the same sum. The bitmask DP approach efficiently explores all possible partitions using bitmask representation of element selections. We first check if total sum is divisible by k; if not, return false immediately. Let target = total_sum/k. We use DP[mask] to store the sum of the current subset modulo target when elements in mask are used. DP[0] = 0 (empty mask). For each mask from 0 to (1<<n)-1, if DP[mask] is defined (not -1), we try adding each unused element (bit not set in mask) to the current subset. If adding it doesn't exceed target, we update DP[new_mask] = (DP[mask] + nums[i]) % target. If it exactly reaches target, we set DP[new_mask] = 0 to start a new subset. The solution exists if DP[(1<<n)-1] == 0, meaning all elements used and last subset completed.

Intuition: Bitmask represents which elements are already assigned to subsets. DP[mask] tracks how 'full' the current subset is (remainder when sum modulo target). This avoids explicitly tracking which subset each element goes to, focusing only on whether we can fill subsets sequentially. The modulo operation ensures we reset when a subset is complete.

Algorithm:
1. Compute total_sum. If total_sum % k != 0, return false.
2. target = total_sum / k.
3. Initialize DP array of size 2^n with -1 (unreachable). DP[0] = 0.
4. For mask from 0 to (1<<n)-1:
   If DP[mask] != -1:
     For i from 0 to n-1:
       If mask & (1<<i) == 0 (element not used):
         new_mask = mask | (1<<i)
         new_sum = DP[mask] + nums[i]
         If new_sum <= target:
           DP[new_mask] = new_sum % target
5. Return DP[(1<<n)-1] == 0

Time: O(n * 2^n) - n elements, 2^n masks.
Space: O(2^n) for DP array.

Connection to L1: This uses the DP state transition as the key subroutine.

Common mistakes: Not checking divisibility first, incorrect DP initialization, forgetting modulo reset when sum equals target, not handling large n (n <= 15-20 for bitmask).

Real code pattern:
def canPartitionKSubsets(nums, k):
    total = sum(nums)
    if total % k != 0: return False
    target = total // k
    n = len(nums)
    dp = [-1] * (1 << n)
    dp[0] = 0
    for mask in range(1 << n):
        if dp[mask] != -1:
            for i in range(n):
                if not (mask & (1 << i)):
                    new_mask = mask | (1 << i)
                    new_sum = dp[mask] + nums[i]
                    if new_sum <= target:
                        dp[new_mask] = new_sum % target
    return dp[(1 << n) - 1] == 0

examples.trace:
total_sum = 20, target = 5, n=7, DP size=128
Mask 0: dp=0
  Add nums[0]=4: new_mask=1, new_sum=4 <=5, dp[1]=4
  Add nums[1]=3: new_mask=2, new_sum=3, dp[2]=3
  Add nums[2]=2: new_mask=4, new_sum=2, dp[4]=2
  Add nums[3]=3: new_mask=8, new_sum=3, dp[8]=3
  Add nums[4]=5: new_mask=16, new_sum=5, dp[16]=0 (reset)
  Add nums[5]=2: new_mask=32, new_sum=2, dp[32]=2
  Add nums[6]=1: new_mask=64, new_sum=1, dp[64]=1
Mask 1 (dp=4):
  Add nums[1]=3: new_mask=3, new_sum=7 >5 skip
  Add nums[2]=2: new_mask=5, new_sum=6 >5 skip
  Add nums[3]=3: new_mask=9, new_sum=7 >5 skip
  Add nums[4]=5: new_mask=17, new_sum=9 >5 skip
  Add nums[5]=2: new_mask=33, new_sum=6 >5 skip
  Add nums[6]=1: new_mask=65, new_sum=5, dp[65]=0
... continue transitions...
Final mask 127 (all bits set): dp[127]=0, return true
Valid partition: [5], [4,1], [3,2], [3,2] all sum to 5

total_sum = 10, 10%3=1 !=0, return false immediately
No need DP computation

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP State Transition with Bitmask
ladders.desc: Implement the core DP transition that builds subsets using bitmask representation.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine focuses on the DP state transition logic: given a current mask representing used elements and a current subset sum remainder, try adding each unused element to build towards completing subsets. The key insight is using modulo target to track progress and reset when a subset is full.

Intuition: Each mask represents a set of elements already assigned to subsets. DP[mask] stores the sum of the 'current' subset modulo target. When we add an element, if the new sum <= target, we update. If new sum == target, we reset to 0 (starting new subset). This sequential filling is valid because subsets are indistinguishable; we just need to fill k subsets of target sum.

Algorithm:
Input: nums array, target sum, current mask, current dp_value
For each i where mask bit i is 0:
  new_mask = mask | (1<<i)
  new_sum = dp_value + nums[i]
  if new_sum <= target:
    dp[new_mask] = new_sum % target

Time: O(n) per mask (checking n elements).
Space: O(1) for transition.

Connection to L0: This transition is the inner loop in the full solution.

Common mistakes: Not checking new_sum <= target before updating, incorrect bit operations, not handling modulo correctly when new_sum == target.

Real code pattern:
def dp_transition(mask, dp_val, nums, target, dp):
    n = len(nums)
    for i in range(n):
        if not (mask & (1 << i)):
            new_mask = mask | (1 << i)
            new_sum = dp_val + nums[i]
            if new_sum <= target:
                dp[new_mask] = new_sum % target

examples.trace:
mask=0 (no elements used), dp_val=0
Check i=0 (nums[0]=2): bit 0 not set, new_mask=001, new_sum=0+2=2 <=3, dp[001]=2%3=2
Check i=1 (nums[1]=3): new_mask=010, new_sum=3 <=3, dp[010]=3%3=0 (reset)
Check i=2 (nums[2]=1): new_mask=100, new_sum=1 <=3, dp[100]=1%3=1
Updated dp for masks 1,2,4

mask=001 (element 0 used), dp_val=2
Check i=1: bit 1 not set, new_mask=011, new_sum=2+3=5 >3 skip
Check i=2: bit 2 not set, new_mask=101, new_sum=2+1=3 <=3, dp[101]=3%3=0
Only update mask 101

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bitmask Representation of Subsets
ladders.desc: Using bitmask integers to represent subsets of array elements.
ladders.explanation:
Bitmask representation uses an integer where each bit corresponds to whether an array element is included in a subset. For n elements, we use n-bit masks from 0 to (1<<n)-1. Bit i is 1 if element i is in subset, 0 otherwise. This allows efficient subset iteration and DP state representation.

Intuition: Instead of storing explicit lists of elements, we use compact integer representation. Each mask uniquely identifies a subset. Operations: check if element i is in mask (mask & (1<<i)), add element i (mask | (1<<i)), iterate over all masks from 0 to (1<<n)-1. This is efficient for n up to ~20 (2^20 ≈ 1 million masks).

Algorithm:
Let n = len(nums)
Total masks = 1 << n
For mask in range(total_masks):
  For i in range(n):
    if mask & (1 << i): # element i in subset
      process element i

Time: O(2^n * n) to iterate all masks and check bits.
Space: O(1) for mask storage.

Connection to L1: Bitmask is used as DP state key.

Common mistakes: Off-by-one in mask range (should be < total_masks), incorrect bit shifting (1<<i not i<<1), misunderstanding bit order (LSB = element 0).

Real code pattern:
n = len(nums)
total_masks = 1 << n
for mask in range(total_masks):
    subset = []
    for i in range(n):
        if mask & (1 << i):
            subset.append(nums[i])
    # process subset

examples.trace:
Mask 0 (000): subset []
Mask 1 (001): subset [5] (bit 0 set)
Mask 2 (010): subset [2] (bit 1 set)
Mask 3 (011): subset [5,2] (bits 0,1 set)
Mask 4 (100): subset [3] (bit 2 set)
Mask 5 (101): subset [5,3]
Mask 6 (110): subset [2,3]
Mask 7 (111): subset [5,2,3]

examples.input: Check element 1 in mask 5 for nums=[5,2,3]
examples.trace:
mask=5 (binary 101), i=1 (second element)
Check mask & (1<<1): 101 & 010 = 000 = 0, so element 1 not in subset
Subset is [5,3]

ladders.title: Building Block — Subset Sum Modulo Target
ladders.desc: Tracking sum of subset modulo target sum to manage multiple subsets.
ladders.explanation:
When partitioning into k subsets of target sum, we need to track progress filling subsets. Instead of storing which subset each element belongs to, we store current subset sum modulo target. When sum reaches target, we reset to 0 (start new subset). This works because subsets are indistinguishable; we just need to fill k buckets.

Intuition: Imagine filling subsets sequentially. We maintain current_sum of the subset we're currently filling. When we add an element, if current_sum + element <= target, we update. If it equals target, we reset to 0 (subset full, move to next). The modulo operation automatically handles reset: (current_sum + element) % target gives remainder, which is 0 when sum equals target.

Algorithm:
Let target = total_sum / k
Initialize current_sum = 0
For each element in subset:
  if current_sum + element <= target:
    current_sum = (current_sum + element) % target
  else:
    invalid (can't add element)
If final current_sum == 0, subset can be partitioned.

Time: O(m) for m elements in subset.
Space: O(1).

Connection to L2: This modulo tracking is stored in DP[mask] value.

Common mistakes: Not checking <= target before adding, misunderstanding modulo behavior with target, not resetting properly when sum==target.

Real code pattern:
def update_sum(current, element, target):
    new_sum = current + element
    if new_sum <= target:
        return new_sum % target
    else:
        return -1  # invalid

examples.trace:
start_sum=0
Add 2: new_sum=2 <=5, sum=2%5=2
Add 1: new_sum=3 <=5, sum=3%5=3
Add 2: new_sum=5 <=5, sum=5%5=0
Final sum=0, valid subset

start_sum=0
Add 3: new_sum=3 <=5, sum=3%5=3
Add 3: new_sum=6 >5, invalid, cannot add

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bit Manipulation for Set Operations
ladders.desc: Using bitwise operators to manipulate sets represented as bitmasks.
ladders.explanation:
Bitwise operations allow efficient manipulation of sets represented as bitmasks. Key operations: AND (&) to check membership, OR (|) to add elements, left shift (<<) to create bit positions. For n elements, we use n-bit integers where bit i corresponds to element i.

Intuition: Each bit in an integer can represent true/false for element inclusion. Bitwise operations are fast and concise. Common patterns:
- Check if element i in set: mask & (1<<i) != 0
- Add element i to set: mask | (1<<i)
- Remove element i: mask & ~(1<<i)
- Iterate all subsets: for mask in range(1<<n)

Algorithm:
Let n = number of elements
Create bit for element i: 1 << i
Check membership: if mask & (1<<i):
Add element: mask = mask | (1<<i)

Time: O(1) per operation.
Space: O(1) for mask storage.

Connection to L3: Bitmask is used to represent which elements are in current subset.

Common mistakes: Using wrong operator (e.g., + instead of |), off-by-one in shift (1<<i vs i<<1), not using parentheses around shift.

Real code pattern:
def bit_operations():
    n = 3
    mask = 0
    # Add element 1
    mask = mask | (1 << 1)  # mask becomes 2 (binary 010)
    # Check element 0
    if mask & (1 << 0):  # false
        print("element 0 present")
    # Add element 2
    mask = mask | (1 << 2)  # mask becomes 6 (110)

examples.input: n=3, initial mask=0, add elements 0 and 2
examples.trace:
initial mask=0 (000)
Add element 0: mask = 0 | (1<<0) = 0 | 1 = 1 (001)
Add element 2: mask = 1 | (1<<2) = 1 | 4 = 5 (101)
Check element 1: 5 & (1<<1) = 5 & 2 = 0, not present

mask=3 (11)
Check element 0: 3 & (1<<0) = 3 & 1 = 1 !=0, present
Check element 1: 3 & (1<<1) = 3 & 2 = 2 !=0, present

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Subsets and Sums
ladders.desc: Basic idea of subsets and calculating their sums.
ladders.explanation:
A subset is a selection of elements from a set, possibly empty. For an array of numbers, each subset has a sum (total of its elements). This foundational concept involves enumerating subsets and computing sums.

Intuition: Given array [a,b,c], subsets include [], [a], [b], [c], [a,b], [a,c], [b,c], [a,b,c]. Each subset's sum is sum of its elements. This is basic combinatorics and arithmetic.

Algorithm:
Let nums be array of length n
For each subset (can be represented by indices):
  sum = 0
  For each index i in subset:
    sum += nums[i]
  Record sum

Time: O(2^n * m) where m is subset size, but typically O(n * 2^n).
Space: O(1) for sum calculation.

Connection to L4: Subsets are represented using bitmasks for efficiency.

Common mistakes: Confusing subsets with subarrays (subsets can be non-contiguous), forgetting empty subset (sum=0), off-by-one in iterations.

Real code pattern:
def subset_sums(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    sums = []
    for mask in range(1 << n):
        s = 0
        for i in range(n):
            if mask & (1 << i):
                s += nums[i]
        sums.append(s)
    return sums

examples.trace:
Array [2,3]
Subset []: sum=0
Subset [2]: sum=2
Subset [3]: sum=3
Subset [2,3]: sum=2+3=5

Array [1]
Subset []: sum=0
Subset [1]: sum=1
